note: ORIGINAL paraphrase-test dataset (challenge 7d) for the SOA Exam P Speedrun fork. Each card has a memory prompt (recall the fact) and TWO reworded exam-style questions that test the same idea in new words. These use standard probability results and are NOT copied from any real SOA exam. Held out of all AI training/index/few-shot; distinctness of the rewordings vs the card prompt is enforced by paraphrase.reworded_distinctness.
license_note: Original content, AGPL-3.0-or-later with the rest of the fork.

cards.fact: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B).
cards.card_prompt: State the addition rule for the probability that A or B occurs.
reworded: In a class, 60% study French and 45% study Spanish, while 30% study both. What fraction study at least one of the two languages?
One card is drawn from a standard 52-card deck. What is the chance it is a heart or a face card?

cards.fact: The complement rule: P(not A) = 1 - P(A).
cards.card_prompt: Give the rule relating an event's probability to that of its complement.
reworded: A machine breaks down on a given day with probability 0.02. What is the likelihood it runs the whole day without breaking?
If the chance of at least one flaw in a shipment is 0.13, how likely is a flaw-free shipment?

cards.fact: The count of unordered selections of k from n is C(n,k) = n! / (k! (n-k)!).
cards.card_prompt: Write the formula for the number of ways to choose k objects from n when order does not matter.
reworded: A manager will invite 3 of 10 applicants to interview. How many different interview groups are possible?
A shop offers 8 toppings. How many distinct two-topping pizzas can a customer order?

cards.fact: Distinguishable arrangements of n items = n! divided by the factorial of each repeated group's size.
cards.card_prompt: How do you count the distinct orderings of a word whose letters repeat?
reworded: How many different strings can be formed by rearranging the letters of the word BANANA?
In how many orders can 3 identical red balls and 2 identical blue balls be placed in a row?

cards.fact: For independent events, P(A and B) = P(A) * P(B); multiply probabilities for a joint AND.
cards.card_prompt: State the multiplication rule for the joint occurrence of independent events.
reworded: Three relays each function independently with probability 0.95, and all three must function for the alarm to sound. What is the probability the alarm sounds?
Two fair dice are thrown. How likely is it that both come up six?

cards.fact: For mutually exclusive events, P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B), since they cannot both happen.
cards.card_prompt: How does the addition rule simplify when two events cannot occur together?
reworded: A single die is rolled once. What is the probability the outcome is either a 2 or a 5?
A drum holds red, blue, and green tickets in proportions 0.5, 0.3, and 0.2. What is the chance a drawn ticket is red or green?

cards.fact: Conditional probability: P(A given B) = P(A and B) / P(B) for P(B) > 0.
cards.card_prompt: Define the probability of A conditional on B.
reworded: Among insured drivers, 8% file a claim and 5% both file a claim and are under 25. Of those who file a claim, what fraction are under 25?
A screening turns positive for 12% of patients and is positive-and-diseased for 9%. Given a positive screen, how likely is disease?

cards.fact: Multiplication form of conditioning: P(A and B) = P(A given B) * P(B).
cards.card_prompt: Write the chain rule expressing a joint probability through a conditional one.
reworded: 30% of policies are high-risk, and 40% of high-risk policies lapse. What fraction of all policies are both high-risk and lapse?
A line runs 70% of the time, and while running it makes a defect 3% of the time. What fraction of all time is spent making a defect while running?

cards.fact: Bayes' rule: P(A given B) = P(B given A) P(A) / P(B), with P(B) from total probability.
cards.card_prompt: State Bayes' theorem for revising P(A) after observing B.
reworded: A condition affects 1% of people; a test is 99% sensitive and 95% specific. After a positive result, what is the probability the person has the condition?
Plants A and B make 60% and 40% of units with defect rates 2% and 5%. A unit is defective; how likely did plant B make it?

cards.fact: Law of total probability: P(B) = sum over a partition of P(B given A_i) P(A_i).
cards.card_prompt: How do you find P(B) by conditioning on a partition of the sample space?
reworded: One of two urns is picked with equal chance; they hold 30% and 70% red balls. What is the overall probability of drawing red?
Arrivals are 50% new and 50% returning customers, buying with probabilities 0.1 and 0.3. What share of all arrivals make a purchase?

cards.fact: A valid probability density is nonnegative everywhere and its total integral equals 1.
cards.card_prompt: State the two requirements a probability density function must meet.
reworded: A continuous variable has density proportional to x on the interval from 0 to 2. What constant makes it a legitimate density?
For a density of the form k(1 - x) on the interval 0 to 1, determine k so the probabilities total one.

cards.fact: The cdf F(x) = P(X <= x) is nondecreasing from 0 to 1, and its derivative is the density.
cards.card_prompt: Relate the cumulative distribution function to the density and list its main properties.
reworded: A lifetime has cdf 1 - e^(-x/4) for x >= 0. What is the density at x = 4?
A distribution's cdf jumps from 0.30 to 0.55 at one point. How much probability sits exactly at that point?

cards.fact: Expected value: sum of x times p(x) (discrete) or the integral of x times f(x) (continuous).
cards.card_prompt: Give the definition of the expected value of a random variable.
reworded: A game pays $10 with probability 0.2 and nothing otherwise. What is the long-run average payout per play?
A loss amount has density x/8 on the interval from 0 to 4. What is the mean loss?

cards.fact: Expectation is linear: E[aX + b] = a E[X] + b, and E[X + Y] = E[X] + E[Y] always.
cards.card_prompt: State the linearity property of expectation.
reworded: Claims average $500 each. If every claim is increased by 10% and then a flat $50 is added, what is the new average claim?
Two ordinary dice are rolled. What is the expected value of the total showing?

cards.fact: Variance equals the second moment minus the squared mean: Var(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2.
cards.card_prompt: Give the shortcut formula for variance in terms of moments.
reworded: A variable satisfies E[X] = 3 and E[X^2] = 13. What is its variance?
A bet pays $2 with probability 0.5 and $0 otherwise. Find its variance from the first two moments.

cards.fact: Var(aX + b) = a^2 Var(X); adding a constant does not change spread, scaling multiplies variance by the square.
cards.card_prompt: How does the linear change aX + b affect the variance of X?
reworded: Losses have a standard deviation of $200. If each loss is doubled, what is the variance of the doubled loss?
Readings with variance 9 are transformed by the rule new = 2 * old + 5. What is the variance afterward?

cards.fact: A binomial(n, p) count has mean np and variance np(1 - p).
cards.card_prompt: State the mean and variance of a binomial random variable.
reworded: An agent independently closes each of 20 calls with probability 0.3. What is the expected number of sales and its variance?
A fair coin is tossed 50 times. What is the standard deviation of the number of heads?

cards.fact: A Poisson(lambda) count has mean and variance both equal to lambda.
cards.card_prompt: Give the mean and variance of a Poisson random variable.
reworded: A help desk gets on average 4 calls a minute, following a Poisson law. What is the chance of exactly 2 calls in a given minute?
Flaws appear at an average of 3 per sheet. What is the variance of the number of flaws on one sheet?

cards.fact: An exponential(lambda) has mean 1/lambda and is memoryless: P(X > s + t given X > s) = P(X > t).
cards.card_prompt: State the mean of an exponential distribution and its memoryless property.
reworded: A part's lifetime is exponential with a mean of 5 years. Given it has already lasted 3 years, how likely is it to last 2 more?
Gaps between arrivals average 10 minutes and are exponential. What is the probability of waiting longer than 15 minutes?

cards.fact: A uniform variable on [a, b] has mean (a + b)/2 and variance (b - a)^2 / 12.
cards.card_prompt: Give the mean and variance of a continuous uniform distribution on [a, b].
reworded: A shuttle is equally likely to arrive at any moment within 30 minutes of your arrival. What is your expected wait?
A value is picked uniformly between 2 and 8. What is its variance?

cards.fact: Under an ordinary deductible d, the insurer pays max(X - d, 0), with expected payment the integral from d to infinity of (x - d) f(x) dx.
cards.card_prompt: Express the insurer's payment and its expectation when a deductible d applies.
reworded: Losses are uniform on [0, 1000] and a $200 deductible applies. What is the expected amount the insurer pays per loss?
A policy covers the excess above a $500 deductible; losses are exponential with mean $1000. What is the expected insurer payment?

cards.fact: With a policy limit u, the insurer pays min(X, u): payment is capped at u.
cards.card_prompt: Write the insurer's payment when the policy caps coverage at a maximum u.
reworded: Losses are uniform on [0, 2000] and the policy pays at most $1500. What is the expected payment?
With a coverage cap of $10,000 and losses exponential with mean $8,000, what is the largest amount the insurer could ever pay on one loss?

cards.fact: A joint density is nonnegative and integrates to 1 over the plane; a region's probability is the double integral of the density over that region.
cards.card_prompt: State the conditions for a valid joint density and how to get the probability of a region.
reworded: A joint density is constant on the square where both coordinates lie between 0 and 1. What is that constant?
For a uniform density on the unit square, what is the probability that the two coordinates sum to less than 1?

cards.fact: The marginal density of X integrates the joint density over the other variable: f_X(x) = integral of f(x, y) dy.
cards.card_prompt: How do you recover the marginal distribution of X from a joint density?
reworded: A joint density equals x + y on the unit square. What is the marginal density of the first coordinate?
Starting from a joint density on the unit square, which integration yields the distribution of the second variable by itself?

cards.fact: Expectation of a function of two variables: E[g(X, Y)] = double integral of g(x, y) f(x, y).
cards.card_prompt: Write the expected value of a function of two jointly distributed variables.
reworded: For a uniform density on the unit square, compute the expected value of the product of the two coordinates.
Given a joint density x + y on the unit square, find the expected value of the sum of the two coordinates.

cards.fact: Cov(X, Y) = E[XY] - E[X] E[Y]; it is zero when the variables are independent.
cards.card_prompt: Give the formula for covariance and its value under independence.
reworded: Two variables satisfy E[XY] = 6, E[X] = 2, and E[Y] = 2.5. What is their covariance?
If two random variables are independent, what does their covariance equal?

cards.fact: Correlation = Cov(X, Y) / (sd(X) sd(Y)) and always lies between -1 and 1.
cards.card_prompt: Define the correlation coefficient and state the range of values it can take.
reworded: Two variables have covariance 8 and standard deviations 4 and 5. What is their correlation?
An analyst reports a correlation of 1.4 between two variables. Why must this be a mistake?

cards.fact: For n iid variables with cdf F, the maximum has cdf F(x)^n and the minimum has cdf 1 - (1 - F(x))^n.
cards.card_prompt: Give the distribution functions of the maximum and minimum of n iid variables.
reworded: Three independent parts each have a lifetime uniform on [0, 1]. What is the probability the longest-lasting part exceeds 0.9?
Five independent exponential variables each have mean 1. What is the distribution function of the smallest of the five?

cards.fact: Var(aX + bY) = a^2 Var(X) + b^2 Var(Y) + 2ab Cov(X, Y).
cards.card_prompt: State the variance of a linear combination aX + bY of two variables.
reworded: X and Y have variances 4 and 9 and covariance 2. What is the variance of their sum?
Two assets have variances 0.04 and 0.09 with covariance -0.01. What is the variance of a portfolio holding half of each?

cards.fact: By the central limit theorem, the mean of n iid variables is approximately Normal with mean mu and variance sigma^2 / n for large n.
cards.card_prompt: State what the central limit theorem says about the distribution of a sample mean.
reworded: Claim sizes have mean $400 and standard deviation $200. Approximate the probability that the average of 100 claims exceeds $440.
A die is rolled 200 times. Approximately how is the average of the rolls distributed?
